item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand and appreciate the poetic charisma this document holds, but that doesn't really do much for me. Without it, the SCP itself feels dull and uncreative, and a twist of structure in interview section feels needless except for its aesthetic feeling - in other words, not so practical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beautiful.

I love how you recurrently liken death to a return to the sea, it's primal, powerful stuff. The piece makes you think about death, and how close it is, and how inevitable, yet for all that it is also oddly soothing - there's a peacefulness to death, and something comforting about the fact that we all have to go through it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't usually care for articles like these. But… yeah, I suppose it is quite beautiful, in it's own way. Reluctantly upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm assuming the last poem is written by his late wife. This is wonderful. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted.

Simple and harmless. those kind of scps are rare.

there is a flaw that says the foundation is supposed to be a prision, and yet the subject is in (conditional) freedom even through the fact that he's harmless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the minimalism, and this portrayal of what the Foundation could be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. Just wow.

No, this doesn't fit with my usual model of the Foundation.
No, this doesn't exactly conform to a standard format.

However, that's easily overcome by the beautiful and subtle incorporation of the contest theme, not to mention the moving interview log and poetry.

I consider this the most beautiful piece on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is just… magnificent.

Easily one of the most beautiful pieces of writing I've ever read. And I read a lot!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. Was going to bring this up too!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes! This is exactly how I felt while reading your skip…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only complaint would be the following bit, and, after reading the author commentary, I'm not sure it's unintentional.

The residence of SCP-2911 is sparse and ascetic in nature, the only ornamentation a series of poems hanging from the walls, written at some point after SCP-2911 informs them of their pending demise. Transcriptions follow:

Who are "them?" Like, I know "them" are the people this fella talks to, but that sentence could use some clarification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image file size is 1280 x 1024, but the image in the page is only 300 pixels wide. You should use image editing software to scale the image down to 300 px wide and then re-upload it, so that it will download quicker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is probably my favorite skip of the contest. Both the anomaly and the brevity play into each other to make something really wonderful, the Foundation's low-key, minimalistic (yet effective containment) works wonderfully, and the story plays out in a way that, despite bordering on a tale, works perfectly fine in the SCP format. This isn't a case of the SCP format limiting the narrative at all, even if a tale based on this guy would be wonderful.

What really gets me is the interview. Todoroki and Mikio interact with each other as if they're friends, yet still have to be formal about the whole affair. There's humanity in this that is so scarce throughout most of the other mainlisters on the site and it's wonderful to read about. And it's believable, too.

One thing I noticed, and I'm not sure if this is intentional due to these poems being translated into English, but some of the lines don't exactly fit the format. I'm not sure if you wrote them in Japanese first and then translated, or just wrote the English first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I upvoted regardless. That said, the reasons you present here did run through my head, and I'm willing to believe that you had narrative in mind foremost. :) It certainly helps keep that consistent tone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That said, I do dislike the minimalist interview for the sake of fitting the word count.

Point of order. There's a reason the interview is the way it is for a narrative purpose, not word count.

+ long winded explanation
- long winded explanation

The researcher that is conducting the interview has included her notes, not an interview transcript, into the file. This is for several reasons. She did not bring a recording device, so she is essentially transcribing her notes from the encounter. She also is not in the habit of recording extraneous information, due to her personality. She focuses on what she considers the important points of something and excludes all else.

This is why, by the way, she has been assigned to this SCP. The Foundation, for reasons I sort of went into already, is not a prison in this universe unless it absolutely must be. Chances are, an old guy who keeps to himself in a mountainous, remote fishing village in rural Japan is not going to be threatening consensus reality anytime soon. So they put some minimal safeguards in place, and containment and research is largely left to a researcher assigned to build trust with the subject and to generally keep tabs on things. Dr. Todoroki was chosen because this Foundation makes a practice of assigning researchers for human subjects based on how well they will be able to work with them. Mikio and Dr. Todoroki share a certain outlook, which is why the interview gets a little informal at times.

It also is an intentional choice on my part to stay on a theme of minimalism, which was one I chose based on the contest, but which I made narrative choices in service to, rather than an arbitrary word count. The skip is a minimal threat. The man lives a simple life. His friends leave brief monuments. The researcher sticks to the important points. Etc.

As always, if it doesn't work, then it doesn't work for you. That's fine. But the interview is a stylistic choice, not an attempt to shoehorn something in under the limit. It always looked that way, even in earlier, longer drafts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is kind of your average feels article, but the tone is consistent and it's just so earnest and peaceful, I can't help liking it. That said, I do dislike the minimalist interview for the sake of fitting the word count.

This is was a tricky upvote.

On one hand, I quite like Mikio. His ability may not be the most original thing ever, but the way he uses it is what matters.

The poems had me a bit on the fence about whether to vote or not. The point of comparison is Joaquín and the stories told about him by the agent. Those are obviously essential to understanding of how the anomalies related to him went through. These poems… well, in-universe they are a bit extraneous: they explain nothing about his anomaly. But from a narrative standpoint, they reveal a lot about how he affected these other people. It's more subtle, but I think it does work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is essentially my thoughts. It's an interesting story, and the poetry is nice, but I never really felt it 'clicked' with the SCP format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is life a dream?

Thirding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this. It's quite unique, from my perspective, but then again, I haven't read too many things on the site, so it could be a re-do of something else. Regardless, I found it to be very beautiful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same. It's not doing enough to distinguish itself in that regard.

Also, the… feeling? tone? environment? the sort of general vibe this gives off feels played-out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for being too similar to SCP-657.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In regards to the poetry, it's unavoidable (unless the author went out of the way to get expert opinion). Anyway, as English poems wrapped in a Japanese form, they are acceptable works of poetry.

I'm not going to go too far into what ultimately will be a difference in how I perceive this iteration of the Foundation, but my thought is that the Foundation views a woman from the local "university" coming to visit a harmless old eccentric once a month or so being far less conspicuous and disruptive (and less cost-intensive) than mass relocation.

Unconventional indeed, but I can respect that. Normalcy may be best preserved if there are no real observable alterations to one's daily lives (be it anomalous or non-anomalous). The villagers are used to an old man predicting deaths, then so be it.

I do enjoy a non-intrusive take on the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because this will likely come up again, almost all of the poems are katauta. The last poem is a chōka. There are some inherent issues because the poems are written in English, but they're meant to be from native Japanese speakers. So there's some inherent lapses in logic, which I've decided to just roll with.

Why not vacate all houses within the area of effect and monitor the people whose deaths are predicted?

I'm not going to go too far into what ultimately will be a difference in how I perceive this iteration of the Foundation, but my thought is that the Foundation views a woman from the local "university" coming to visit a harmless old eccentric once a month or so being far less conspicuous and disruptive (and less cost-intensive) than mass relocation. Once could make an argument, logically, for "jail the shit out of everyone," and indeed I've written articles prior from that perspective. I choose to take a different direction here.

Thanks for reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pros:

  • I like the characterisation. It encompasses aspects of ukiyo (floating world).
  • I like the translated poems. While they feel haiku-ish, they do not adhere to the supposed format. But then again, these are translations of Japanese. I will leave it to those versed in Japanese to judge if they can pass off as a legit Japanese style of poetry (be it haiku, waka or tanka).
  • EDIT: In light of Kalinin's explanation, I voice my enjoyment of a non-intrusive Foundation as an unique feature. My con (addressing the lacklustre procedures) is still valid though. It's more of me buying the spirit of it, than its implementation here.

Cons:

  • While the effect is limited to a range, I don't really see the relevance of the procedures. Why not vacate all houses within the area of effect and monitor the people whose deaths are predicted?

Verdict: Upvote

Side note: While the interview format used here is "unconventional", I'm not bothered by it since it's clear who is the interviewer and interviewee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The poetry in this is absolutely beautiful, but… the execution of this feels off. The beauty doesn't quite make up for that. I would love to see this converted into a tale, but as an SCP object it doesn't work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote.

It's a very beautiful little piece, but I don't feel like it is a good fit for the site or the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the formatting is wrong. An interview would be in a quoted section and the names I'd feel would be blocked out. I don't feel that a humanoid SCP would be allowed freedom like this one has. It is flat out not contained. Media supression and let everyone consider him to be folklore isn't containment. I don't think this would be a subject worthy of containment.
